content: Chemotherapy refers to cytotoxic drugs that kill rapidly dividing cells by interfering with cell division. Cell cycle phases: G0 (resting), G1 (growth), S (DNA synthesis), G2 (preparation), M (mitosis). Chemotherapy classes by mechanism: alkylating agents (cyclophosphamide, cisplatin) cross-link DNA, cell cycle non-specific; antimetabolites (5-FU, methotrexate, gemcitabine) mimic nucleotides, S-phase specific; antitumor antibiotics (doxorubicin, bleomycin) intercalate DNA or generate free radicals; plant alkaloids (vincristine, paclitaxel) disrupt microtubules, M-phase specific; topoisomerase inhibitors (etoposide, irinotecan) prevent DNA unwinding. Combination chemotherapy uses drugs with different mechanisms and non-overlapping toxicities to maximize tumor kill while minimizing resistance. Dose intensity (mg/m²/week) correlates with outcomes in many cancers.
